title: W4 A Sequences
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 4A    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/19
 Sequences and Limits




                       2/19
                 Sequences of Real Numbers




Definition 1

 A sequence of real numbers is a function mapping N into R.

                     Sequences of Real Numbers

 Definition 1

   A sequence of real numbers is a function mapping N into R.


Express a sequence of real numbers as
                                  k ∞
                                 x k=1 .

 Example 1

    (a) {wk }∞              k
             k=1 such that w = c for all k ∈ N.

   (b) {xk }∞              k                     k
            k=1 such that x = 0 if k is odd and x = 1 if k is even.

    (c) {y k }∞              k
              k=1 such that y = k.

   (d) {z k }∞              k  1
             k=1 such that z = k .




                                                                      3/19
                       Convergent Sequences


Definition 2

 The sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                   k=1 converges to x ∈ R if for

 every ϵ > 0 there exists some positive integer K such that
                     k ≥ K =⇒ |xk − x| < ϵ.

 If {xk }∞
         k=1 converges to x, then x is called its limit.




                                                                   4/19
                         Convergent Sequences


 Definition 2

   The sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                     k=1 converges to x ∈ R if for

   every ϵ > 0 there exists some positive integer K such that
                       k ≥ K =⇒ |xk − x| < ϵ.

   If {xk }∞
           k=1 converges to x, then x is called its limit.




Continuing with Example 1:
(a) {wk }∞                          k
         k=1 converges to c; write w → c.


(b) {xk }∞
         k=1 alternates between 0 and 1 (it does not have a limit).


(c) {y k }∞                    k
          k=1 diverges, write y → ∞.




                                                                      4/19
                            Example 1 (d)

Claim. The sequence {z k }∞
                          k=1 converges to 0:


                              lim z k = 0.
                             k→∞

  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0.

  ◦ Let K ∈ N be such that K > ? .

  ◦ For all k ≥ K we obtain

                   |z k − z| =    1
                                  k
                                      − 0     =   1
                                                  k

                                                              < ϵ.

  ◦ So, for ϵ > 0, choosing K > 1/ϵ shows that z k → z.

  ◦ Take for example K = 2/ϵ.

                      |z k − z| =     1
                                      k
                                          ≤   1
                                              K

                                                                     5/19
                           Limits are Unique

 Proposition 1

   If the sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                        k=1 converges, then its limit is

   unique.



Proof   I argue by contradiction.
 ◦ Suppose xk → x and xk → y, with x ̸= y.




                                                                           6/19
                           Limits are Unique

 Proposition 1

   If the sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                        k=1 converges, then its limit is

   unique.



Proof   I argue by contradiction.
 ◦ Suppose xk → x and xk → y, with x ̸= y.

 ◦ Fix 0 < ϵ < 12 |x − y|.   ϵ is less than half the distance between x and y.




                                                                                 6/19
                           Limits are Unique

 Proposition 1

   If the sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                        k=1 converges, then its limit is

   unique.



Proof   I argue by contradiction.
 ◦ Suppose xk → x and xk → y, with x ̸= y.

 ◦ Fix 0 < ϵ < 12 |x − y|.   ϵ is less than half the distance between x and y.

 ◦ Let Kx be such that k ≥ Kx implies |xk − x| < ϵ.

 ◦ Let Ky be similarly defined.




                                                                                 6/19
                           Limits are Unique

 Proposition 1

   If the sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                        k=1 converges, then its limit is

   unique.



Proof   I argue by contradiction.
 ◦ Suppose xk → x and xk → y, with x ̸= y.

 ◦ Fix 0 < ϵ < 12 |x − y|.   ϵ is less than half the distance between x and y.

 ◦ Let Kx be such that k ≥ Kx implies |xk − x| < ϵ.

 ◦ Let Ky be similarly defined.

 ◦ Now take K = max{Kx , Ky } and compute the distance between x and
   y again.

                        converges to 1.

                                             7/19
                             Example 2 (a)


Suppose against our better judgement that ak → a, for some a ∈ R.
  ◦ For ϵ = 1, there is some K such that k ≥ K implies |(−1)k − a| < 1.




                                                                          8/19
                             Example 2 (a)


Suppose against our better judgement that ak → a, for some a ∈ R.
  ◦ For ϵ = 1, there is some K such that k ≥ K implies |(−1)k − a| < 1.

  ◦ Note that |(−1)k − (−1)k+1 | = 2, for all k ∈ N.




                                                                          8/19
                              Example 2 (a)


Suppose against our better judgement that ak → a, for some a ∈ R.
  ◦ For ϵ = 1, there is some K such that k ≥ K implies |(−1)k − a| < 1.

  ◦ Note that |(−1)k − (−1)k+1 | = 2, for all k ∈ N.

  ◦ Thus, for k ≥ K,
                  2 = |(−1)k − (−1)k+1 |

                       = |(−1)k − a + a − (−1)k+1 |

                       ≤ |(−1)k − a| + |a − (−1)k+1 |




                                                                          8/19
                              Example 2 (a)


Suppose against our better judgement that ak → a, for some a ∈ R.
  ◦ For ϵ = 1, there is some K such that k ≥ K implies |(−1)k − a| < 1.

  ◦ Note that |(−1)k − (−1)k+1 | = 2, for all k ∈ N.

  ◦ Thus, for k ≥ K,
                  2 = |(−1)k − (−1)k+1 |

                       = |(−1)k − a + a − (−1)k+1 |

                       ≤ |(−1)k − a| + |a − (−1)k+1 | < 2.

                                                              9/19
                          A Useful Observation


Intuitively, if the limit of a sequence {xk }∞
                                             k=1 is x, then the distance

between xk and x becomes negligible as k becomes large.

 Proposition 2

   The sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                     k=1 converges to x ∈ R if, and only

    if,
                             lim xk − x = 0.
                            k→∞




                                                                           10/19
                           A Useful Observation


Intuitively, if the limit of a sequence {xk }∞
                                             k=1 is x, then the distance

between xk and x becomes negligible as k becomes large.

 Proposition 2

   The sequence of real numbers {xk }∞
                                     k=1 converges to x ∈ R if, and only

    if,
                              lim xk − x = 0.
                             k→∞




Proof     The equivalence follows directly from definitions.
  ◦ The sequence {|xk − x|}∞
                           k=1 converges to 0 if for any ϵ > 0 there is a

     positive integer K such that for all k ≥ K, one has

                         |xk − x| − 0 = xk − x < ϵ.

  ◦ This is equivalent to saying that xk → x.


                                                                            10/19
 Properties of Limits of Sequences




                                    11/19
                          Limits Behave Well


Consider two sequences {xk }∞         k ∞
                            k=0 and {y }k=0 in R, with limits x and y:

xk → x and y k → y.
  ◦ Construct the sequence {z k }∞               k   k   k
                                 k=1 by setting z = x + y , for each k ∈ N.

  ◦ What can we say about the limit of {z k }∞
                                             k=1 ?




                                                                              12/19
                           Limits Behave Well


Consider two sequences {xk }∞         k ∞
                            k=0 and {y }k=0 in R, with limits x and y:

xk → x and y k → y.
  ◦ Construct the sequence {z k }∞               k   k   k
                                 k=1 by setting z = x + y , for each k ∈ N.

  ◦ What can we say about the limit of {z k }∞
                                             k=1 ?



 Proposition 3

    (a) xk + y k → x + y.

    (b) α xk → α x, for all α ∈ R.

    (c) xk y k → x y.
                                                              k
    (d) If in addition y k ̸= 0 for all k ∈ N and y ̸= 0, then xyk →   x
                                                                       y
                                                                         .

                         Limits Behave Well



Proof of part (a)
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

                  |xk − x| <   ϵ
                               2
                                    and     |y k − y| <   ϵ
                                                          2
                                                            .

                         Limits Behave Well



Proof of part (a)
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

                  |xk − x| <   ϵ
                               2
                                    and      |y k − y| <   ϵ
                                                           2
                                                             .

  ◦ Then for all k ≥ K, one has

                |(xk + y k ) − (x + y)| ≤ |xk − x| + |y k − y|




                                                                 13/19
                         Limits Behave Well



Proof of part (a)
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be such that for al k ≥ K, we have

                  |xk − x| <   ϵ
                               2
                                    and       |y k − y| <   ϵ
                                                            2
                                                              .

  ◦ Then for all k ≥ K, one has

                |(xk + y k ) − (x + y)| ≤ |xk − x| + |y k − y|
                                          ϵ
                                      <   2
                                              + 2ϵ = ϵ.

  ◦ Thus xk + y k → x + y, as desired.




                                                                  13/19
                             Limits Behave Well


Proof of part (b)
If α = 0, the sequence {α xk }∞
                              k=1 has constant value of zero.

Assume now that α ̸= 0.
  ◦ Fix ϵ > 0 and let K be a positive integer so that, for k ≥ K, one has

                                |xk − x| <      ϵ
                                               |α|
                                                   .

  ◦ For all such k, we can write

                         α xk − α x = |α| |xk − x| < ϵ.



Part (c) is left as exercise. Part (d) is a bit more difficult so we’ll take it for
granted.




                                                                                      14/19
                             Limits Extend Well


Consider the sequence {xkj }∞
                            k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.




                                                                                   15/19
                               Limits Extend Well


Consider the sequence {xkj }∞
                            k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.


Write xk = (xk1 , xk2 , . . . , xkn ) ∈ Rn , and say that xk → x if and only if
xkj → xj for each j = 1, . . . , n.




                                                                                   15/19
                               Limits Extend Well


Consider the sequence {xkj }∞
                            k=1 in R, with limit xj , for each j = 1, . . . , n.


Write xk = (xk1 , xk2 , . . . , xkn ) ∈ Rn , and say that xk → x if and only if
xkj → xj for each j = 1, . . . , n.


 Proposition 4

     (a) xk + y k → x + y.
    (b) α xk → α x, for all α ∈ R.
     (c) xk · y k → x · y.


The proof of this proposition follows from previous results. Note that part (c) deals
with a sequence of real numbers.




                                                                                        15/19
 Closed Sets and Sequences




                            16/19
                     Another Look at Closed Sets


Sometimes it is convenient to use sequences when examining the
topological properties of a set.


 Proposition 5

   The set V ⊆ Rn is closed if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞
                                                                 k=1 such

   that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . ., if xk → x then x ∈ V .




                                                                            17/19
                     Another Look at Closed Sets


Sometimes it is convenient to use sequences when examining the
topological properties of a set.


 Proposition 5

   The set V ⊆ Rn is closed if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞
                                                                 k=1 such

   that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . ., if xk → x then x ∈ V .



 Example 3

   V = [0, 1] and {xk = 1/k : k = 1, 2, . . .}. The limit of the sequence
   {xk } is 0, which belongs to V .




                                                                            17/19
                         Proof of Proposition 5


=⇒ Suppose that V is closed and let {xk } be a sequence with xk ∈ V for all k.
By contradiction, suppose xk → x but x ∈
                                       / V.




                                                                                 18/19
                           Proof of Proposition 5


=⇒ Suppose that V is closed and let {xk } be a sequence with xk ∈ V for all k.
By contradiction, suppose xk → x but x ∈
                                       / V.
  ◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

  ◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

  ◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.




                                                                                   18/19
                           Proof of Proposition 5


=⇒ Suppose that V is closed and let {xk } be a sequence with xk ∈ V for all k.
By contradiction, suppose xk → x but x ∈
                                       / V.
  ◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

  ◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

  ◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.

 ⇐= Suppose that for every sequence {xk } such that xk ∈ V for all k, xk → x
implies x ∈ V . By contradiction, suppose that V is not closed.




                                                                                   18/19
                            Proof of Proposition 5


=⇒ Suppose that V is closed and let {xk } be a sequence with xk ∈ V for all k.
By contradiction, suppose xk → x but x ∈
                                       / V.
  ◦ Since x ∈ V c , and V c is open, there exists ϵ > 0 such that Bϵ (x) ⊆ V c .

  ◦ For such ϵ, there is K such that k ≥ K implies ∥xk − x∥ < ϵ.

  ◦ Thus xk ∈ V c for all k ≥ K, a contradiction.

 ⇐= Suppose that for every sequence {xk } such that xk ∈ V for all k, xk → x
implies x ∈ V . By contradiction, suppose that V is not closed.
  ◦ Then V c is not open.

  ◦ There exists some x̂ ∈ V c such that for each k = 1, 2, . . . one can find x̂k for
    which ∥x̂k − x̂∥ < k1 and x̂k ∈
                                  / V c.

  ◦ Then x̂k ∈ V for each k and x̂k → x̂, yet x̂ ∈
                                                 / V.




                                                                                         18/19
                  Another Look at Bounded Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use subsequences when examining
topological properties of a set.

 Proposition 6

   The set V ⊆ Rn is bounded if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞
                                                                  k=1

   such that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . . , there exists a subsequence
   {xkj }∞                 n
         j=1 and some x ∈ R such that x
                                        kj
                                           → x.




                                                                           19/19
                  Another Look at Bounded Sets

Sometimes it is convenient to use subsequences when examining
topological properties of a set.

 Proposition 6

   The set V ⊆ Rn is bounded if and only if for any sequence {xk }∞
                                                                  k=1

   such that xk ∈ V for all k = 1, 2, . . . , there exists a subsequence
   {xkj }∞                 n
         j=1 and some x ∈ R such that x
                                        kj
                                           → x.


 Example 4

   Let V = [−1, 1] and consider the sequence {xk } given by xk = (−1)k .
   This sequence does not converge, but it has convergent subsequences:

             x2j = 1 → 1        and     x2j−1 = −1 → −1.
